context: Items where Gemini passes disagreed. Review and classify.

items.text: The Nikola Tesla Museum is located in Belgrade, on Krunska Street.
items.gemini_reasoning: This is a verifiable geographical and institutional fact.

items.text: The baptismal records at the Nikola Tesla Museum are kept behind heavy, UV-filtering glass, which serves to hold the paper flat, contrary to curators' claim it protects ink from fading.
items.gemini_reasoning: The specific details about the baptismal records, the perceived and actual purpose of the glass, and the contradiction of the curators' claim are presented as facts within the documentary's narrative but are elements of its invented lore.

items.text: The curators at the Nikola Tesla Museum say the glass protects the ink from fading.
items.gemini_reasoning: This describes the stated belief or explanation of the curators.

items.text: The page for eighteen fifty-six in the baptismal records is warped, ripples near the bottom, as if soaked in water and dried in a freezing room, and has dark ink pressed so hard it bruised the paper.
items.gemini_reasoning: These highly specific, dramatic, and unusual physical details about a historical document are presented as facts within the narrative but are clearly invented elements of the lore.

items.text: as if the vellum was once soaked in water and allowed to dry in a freezing room
items.gemini_reasoning: This is a vivid simile used to describe the warped page.

items.text: Nikola Tesla's parents were Milutin and Duka.
items.gemini_reasoning: This is a verifiable biographical detail about Nikola Tesla.

items.text: The entry in the baptismal records for July 1856, for Nikola, son of Milutin and Duka, was recorded by a priest named Father Toma.
items.gemini_reasoning: While Tesla's parents and birth year are historical, the specific priest, Father Toma, and his role in this particular entry are part of the invented lore.

items.text: The handwriting for Nikola's name in the baptismal records is shaky, with jagged loops, and the pen nib snagged on the letter N, tearing a microscopic hole.
items.gemini_reasoning: These minute, dramatic details about the handwriting are fabricated elements of the narrative.

items.text: The handwriting for Nikola's name in the baptismal records indicates the writer (Father Toma) was not looking at the page, but rather looking over his shoulder.
items.gemini_reasoning: This interprets the physical characteristic of the handwriting as a reflection of the character's internal state or fear.

items.text: In the margin of the baptismal record, reserved for notes on temperament, there is a smear of ink, possibly a thumbprint or a smudge from someone trying to wipe away a mistake.
items.gemini_reasoning: These specific, evocative details about the margin are invented narrative elements.

items.text: Underneath the smear, carved into the grain with a force that almost tore the page, is the Serbian phrase 'Dolazi izmedu', meaning 'Arriving from between'.
items.gemini_reasoning: The carved phrase and its translation are central, invented elements of the lore.

items.text: Father Toma wrote the Serbian phrase 'Dolazi izmedu' and closed the book, after which Nikola Tesla's baptism was his last.
items.gemini_reasoning: This connects Father Toma's action directly to the phrase and his last baptism, reinforcing the lore.

items.text: Father Toma claimed a palsy of the hand and that his fingers would no longer hold the quill.
items.gemini_reasoning: This describes Father Toma's stated reason for his inability to write.

items.text: Three weeks later, Father Toma requested a transfer to a mountain monastery; however, official records of his transfer show his hand was steady and letters crisp, contradicting his claim of palsy.
items.gemini_reasoning: The details of the transfer, the contradictory handwriting, and the implied mystery are all part of the invented narrative.

items.text: Upon his transfer, Father Toma took a vow of contemplative silence, maintaining it for thirty-five years, and never spoke again.
items.gemini_reasoning: This dramatic life event for Father Toma is an invented narrative detail.

items.text: In the margin of his transfer request, Father Toma inscribed the Latin phrase 'Quod transit debet cito nominari', meaning 'What comes through must be named quickly', as the last words he ever wrote officially.
items.gemini_reasoning: The specific Latin phrase, its translation, and the context of it being Father Toma's last official words are key elements of the invented lore.

The Nikola Tesla Museum in Belgrade, on Krunska Street, keeps the baptismal records.

items.text: The curators say the glass protects the ink from fading. But look closer. The glass is there to hold the paper flat.
items.gemini_reasoning: This presents a narrative twist on the stated purpose of the glass, creating an 'uncovering' of a deeper, non-obvious truth, which is characteristic of the series' blend of real history and invented lore.

items.text: The phrase 'Arriving from between' is mysteriously linked to Nikola Tesla's origin or nature.

Father Toma wrote it, and then he closed the book. The parish records show that Nikola Tesla was his last baptism. He claimed a palsy of the hand. He claimed his fingers would no longer hold the quill.
items.gemini_reasoning: This sequence of events (writing the phrase, it being his last baptism, and the convenient claim of palsy) is a narrative construction to deepen the mystery around Father Toma and Tesla's baptism.
